raw_case:
Tuberculous Tenosynovitis of the Wrist: A Rare Case Report


Abstract



Introduction:

Tuberculosis (TB) of the wrist joint is a rare presentation of extrapulmonary TB, accounting for about 1% of musculoskeletal TB cases. We present a case of wrist joint TB in a 15-year-old immunocompetent male patient.

Case Report:

A 15-year-old boy presented with 6-month history of left wrist swelling and pain along with constitutional symptoms for 2 months. Examination showed swelling, tenderness, and reduced range of wrist motion. Investigations revealed elevated inflammatory markers, lytic lesions on wrist X-ray, and positive polymerase chain reaction ( ) for Mycobacterium tuberculosis in synovial fluid. Histopathology of synovial biopsy demonstrated caseating granulomas, confirming the diagnosis- treatment with 6 months of anti-tubercular therapy led to complete resolution.

Conclusion:

Though rare, wrist joint TB should be a diagnostic consideration in patients with chronic wrist symptoms. A high index of suspicion and early diagnosis using microbiological, radiological, and histopathological investigations prevents morbidity.

Learning Point of the Article:

Highlighting the atypical presentation of tuberculosis in the wrist underscores the importance of considering uncommon locations for the disease. This case emphasizes the need for vigilant clinical evaluation and awareness among healthcare professionals to ensure timely diagnosis and appropriate management, ultimately improving patient outcomes.

Introduction

Tuberculosis (TB) remains a major global public health concern despite significant advances in diagnosis and treatment. In 2019, about 10 million people developed symptomatic TB disease, and 1.4 million died from it worldwide [1]. The etiological agent, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, most commonly affects the lungs, causing pulmonary TB, but can also affect other organs, causing extrapulmonary TB (EPTB) [2]. Around 15 –20% of all TB cases are extrapulmonary, and musculoskeletal sites are the third most common location for EPTB, accounting for 10 –20% of cases after lymph nodes and pleural effusions [3, 4]. Any bone or joint can be involved in musculoskeletal TB, with the weight-bearing spine and large joints being more susceptible.Isolated involvement of the wrist joint is rare and is reported to occur in 1% or fewer cases of skeletal TB [5, 6]. Due to its rarity, atypical presentation, and radiological similarities with other conditions, wrist joint TB poses diagnostic challenges, often delaying timely treatment [7]. We present a case of wrist joint TB in a 15-year-old boy who was successfully treated with anti-tubercular therapy.

Case Report

A 15- year-old boy presented with progressive swelling and pain of the left wrist for 6 months. He also complained of an evening rise in temperature, night sweats, and loss of appetite for 2 months. He denied any history of wrist trauma, TB contact, chronic cough, or treatment for TB in the past. On examination, there was a diffuse, mildly tender swelling of 4 × 4 cm, more pronounced over the dorsal aspect of the left wrist joint (Fig. 1-3). The local temperature was raised, and the overlying skin appeared inflamed. Wrist movements were painfully restricted (flexion/extension range 0-15°). Distal neurovascular status and systemic examination, including chest examination, were unremarkable. Blood counts and biochemical investigations were within the normal range, apart from raised erythrocyte sedimentation rates (ESR) (46 mm/h). Human immunodeficiency virus serology was non-reactive; the Mantoux test was positive (induration 12 mm). X-ray of the wrist joint showed destructive lytic lesions, mainly involving carpal bones and bases of the 2nd –5th metacarpals (Fig. 4). Synovial fluid analysis revealed turbid fluid with protein of 4.5 g /dL, glucose of 25 mg/dL (blood glucose 98 mg/dL), leucocytes 1200/cumm (90% neutrophils), and no crystals. The Ziehl-Neelsen (ZN) stain did not show acid-fast bacillus, while TB-polymerase chain reaction (PCR) was positive. A decision was made to go ahead with a synovial biopsy, open synovial biopsy was done (Fig. 5). Histology samples showed caseating granulomas comprising epithelioid cells, Langerhans giant cells, and lymphocytic aggregates. The diagnosis of EPTB affecting the carpal bones was made. The patient was started on anti-TB treatment, which was continued for 9 months and eventually stopped as symptoms resolved and subsequent TB-PCR was negative. He was immobilized with a wrist splint and underwent physiotherapy during treatment. At the final follow-up at 9 months, he was asymptomatic with no clinical signs of active disease and normal follow-up X-ray.

Discussion

The musculoskeletal system ranks as the third most prevalent site for TB, following the lungs and lymph nodes, constituting approximately 10 –20% of EPTB cases [1]. Among these, bone TB predominantly affects weight-bearing joints, with a notable impact on large joints, such as the spine, hip, and knee. Wrist TB, a rarity accounting for nearly 1% of EPTB instances, poses diagnostic challenges during the initial assessment. The complexity arises due to multiple potential differentials, including subacute or chronic suppurative arthritis, osteochondrosis, rheumatoid arthritis, Kaposi sarcoma, and benign bone tumors [8], and all the differentials were ruled out in our patient.Patients typically present with non-specific local symptoms such as joint pain, swelling, stiffness, and wound discharge, often accompanied by systemic manifestations, such as fever, night sweats, and weight loss [ 7]. Inflammation commonly results in elevated ESR and C-reactive protein levels, though atypical TB may exhibit normal values. Clinical investigation’s sensitivity varies, with Z N staining showing only 20%, PCR 63%, histological examination 65%, and mycobacterium culture 80% sensitivity [9].Approximately, one-third of wrist TB cases coincide with pulmonary infection, while two-thirds manifest in isolation [10, 11]. Initial evaluation involves plain radiography of the wrist, complemented by a chest radiograph to ascertain concomitant pulmonary TB. Plain wrist radiography typically reveals sclerosis and osteolytic lesions in the carpal and other affected bones, although such findings are non-specific and may mimic conditions, such as pyogenic osteomyelitis, inflammatory arthritis, or malignancy [11]. While computed tomography and Magnetic Resonance Imaging scans lack specificity, they aid in assessing swelling extent, and ultrasound serves as an additional tool for evaluating swelling.Confirmation of diagnosis involves identifying M. tuberculosis in cultures and observing a histological pattern typical for TB. This pattern typically consists of necrotizing granulomatous inflammation characterized by epithelioid histiocytes surrounding a central caseating necrotic zone, accompanied by multinucleated giant cells (Langerhans cells) and lymphocytes [12].Treatment for wrist TB is primarily non-operative, involving a regimen of anti-TB medications. The World Health Organization’s 2019 guidelines recommend a 2-month intensive phase with rifampicin, isoniazid, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide, followed by a 4-month continuation phase with rifampicin and isoniazid [13]. Most cases (approximately 75%) respond successfully to medication, resulting in favorable hand and wrist functional outcomes. Surgery is advised in cases involving nerve compression, impending bone collapse, or the necessity for joint debridement, drainage of large abscesses, or deformity correction in the context of healed disease [13].

Conclusion

TB of the wrist joint is uncommon, constituting about 1% of skeletal TB. Due to the unusual location and vague initial symptoms, diagnosis is often delayed, leading to morbidity. Our case highlights the need for maintaining a high index of suspicion for TB in endemic countries. Targeted investigations exploring cytological, microbiological, radiological, and histological aspects are essential to confirm the diagnosis. Timely and proper treatment ensures excellent outcomes in the majority without the need for surgery. Reporting of such cases will help improve understanding and management of this rare manifestation of EPTB.

Clinical Message

TB tenosynovitis in the wrist is an uncommon manifestation, making this case report valuable for highlighting the diversity of presentations of TB. The rarity of the condition underscores the need for awareness among clinicians. Since pain and swelling of the wrist joint arouse a number of differentials that are much more common compared to TB of the wrist, it can be misdiagnosed, leading to delay in treatment, worsening of conditions, and unnecessary use of drugs. Hence, it is important to bring this condition to light so that physicians can also consider it while diagnosing, especially in TB -prone regions.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 15-year-old male
- Chief Complaint: Swelling and pain in the left wrist for 6 months
- History of Present Illness: Progressive swelling and pain in the left wrist for 6 months, accompanied by constitutional symptoms—fever (evening rise), night sweats, and loss of appetite—for 2 months. No history of wrist trauma, TB contact, chronic cough, or previous TB treatment.
- Past Medical History: None
- Personal History: No history of TB exposure or trauma.
- Family History: None
- Physical Examination: 4×4 cm diffuse, mildly tender swelling on the dorsal aspect of the left wrist. Local temperature raised, overlying skin inflamed. Painful restriction of wrist movements (flexion/extension range 0-15°). Neurovascular status and systemic examination were normal.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Elevated ESR (46 mm/h), normal blood counts, and biochemistry.
  - Mantoux test positive (12 mm induration).
  - X-ray: Lytic lesions of carpal bones and bases of 2nd–5th metacarpals.
  - Synovial fluid: Turbid fluid, protein 4.5 g/dL, glucose 25 mg/dL (blood glucose: 98 mg/dL), white blood cells 1200/cumm (90% neutrophils), ZN stain negative, TB-PCR positive.
  - Histopathology: Caseating granulomas with epithelioid cells, Langerhans giant cells, and lymphocytic aggregates.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Subacute/Chronic Suppurative Arthritis**: Included due to chronic joint swelling, pain, and inflammation, but excluded due to the absence of pus in synovial fluid and confirmation of TB on histopathology (caseating granulomas).
2. **Rheumatoid Arthritis**: Excluded given the absence of systemic joints involvement, serology for rheumatoid factors, and the presence of TB-specific findings on biopsy.
3. **Osteochondrosis and Bone Tumors**: Initially considered due to lytic lesions on X-ray, but excluded due to the confirmed TB-PCR positivity and histological findings.
4. **Pyogenic Osteomyelitis**: Mimicked by chronic inflammatory changes and lytic lesions, but excluded as ZN staining, TB-PCR, and caseating granulomas pointed to TB instead.
5. **Tuberculous Tenosynovitis of the Wrist**: Confirmed due to positive TB-PCR, characteristic caseating granulomas on histopathology, and clinical resolution with anti-TB treatment.
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
- Tuberculous Tenosynovitis of the Wrist  
This was based on clinical complaints of progressive wrist swelling and pain, constitutional symptoms, radiological findings of lytic lesions, positive TB-PCR, and histopathological confirmation of caseating granulomas.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Summarize Key Findings from Patient Information and Chief Complaint:** A 15-year-old male presents with a 6-month history of progressive swelling and pain in his left wrist.

<step 2> **Analyze History of Present Illness:** The patient reports progressive wrist swelling and pain for 6 months. Crucially, he also developed constitutional symptoms (fever, night sweats, loss of appetite) for the past 2 months. The evening rise in fever is a classic symptom suggestive of tuberculosis.  The absence of trauma history makes traumatic causes less likely. The lack of TB contact history or cough doesn't rule out TB, as primary TB can occur without known exposure or pulmonary involvement initially.

<step 3> **Evaluate Physical Examination Findings:** Examination reveals localized findings in the left wrist: swelling, tenderness, inflammation (raised local temperature, inflamed skin), and restricted wrist movement. The size (4x4 cm) and location (dorsal aspect) of the swelling are noted. Normal neurovascular and systemic examination indicates the pathology is likely localized to the wrist without systemic complications or neurovascular compromise at this stage.

<step 4> **Interpret Ancillary Tests - Inflammatory Markers and TB Screening:** Elevated ESR (46 mm/h) confirms systemic inflammation. Normal blood counts and biochemistry are less specific but help rule out other systemic illnesses initially. A positive Mantoux test (12 mm induration) suggests prior exposure to *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and/or latent or active TB infection.  A 12mm induration in a young individual is considered positive.

<step 5> **Interpret Ancillary Tests - Imaging (X-ray):** X-ray showing lytic lesions in carpal bones and metacarpal bases is highly suggestive of a destructive process affecting the bone. In the context of wrist swelling and pain, infectious or neoplastic etiologies should be considered.  Lytic lesions are consistent with tuberculous osteomyelitis or arthritis.

<step 6> **Interpret Ancillary Tests - Synovial Fluid Analysis:** Turbid synovial fluid is indicative of inflammation or infection. High protein (4.5 g/dL) further supports inflammation. Low glucose (25 mg/dL compared to blood glucose 98 mg/dL) is a significant finding, strongly suggesting infection, especially bacterial or tuberculous infection, as microorganisms consume glucose. A high WBC count (1200/cumm) with neutrophil predominance (90%) points towards an inflammatory or infectious process, although the WBC count is not extremely high for a typical bacterial septic arthritis, making tuberculous or other subacute infections more probable. Negative ZN stain does not exclude TB, as it has low sensitivity, especially in paucibacillary conditions like musculoskeletal TB.  However, **positive TB-PCR is a crucial finding, strongly indicating the presence of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* DNA in the synovial fluid and is highly specific for tuberculous infection.**

<step 7> **Interpret Ancillary Tests - Histopathology:** Histopathology revealing caseating granulomas with epithelioid cells, Langerhans giant cells, and lymphocytic aggregates is pathognomonic for tuberculosis. Caseating granulomas are the hallmark histological feature of TB.

<step 8> **Integrate and Synthesize all Findings to Formulate Diagnosis:** Combining the clinical presentation (subacute onset of wrist swelling and pain, constitutional symptoms), physical examination (localized wrist inflammation), and ancillary tests (positive Mantoux, lytic lesions on X-ray, TB-PCR positive synovial fluid, and caseating granulomas on histology), the diagnosis of **Tuberculosis of the left wrist joint (Tuberculous arthritis of the wrist)** is definitively established. All findings are highly consistent with this diagnosis. Other differentials like bacterial septic arthritis are less likely due to the subacute onset, constitutional symptoms, and histopathological findings. While fungal infections or other granulomatous diseases could be considered, the positive TB-PCR and caseating granulomas strongly favor tuberculosis.

### Answer:
Tuberculosis of the left wrist joint (Tuberculous arthritis of the wrist)

A 12mm induration in a young individual is considered positive.

In the context of wrist swelling and pain, infectious or neoplastic etiologies should be considered.  Lytic lesions are consistent with tuberculous osteomyelitis or arthritis.

Low glucose (25 mg/dL compared to blood glucose 98 mg/dL) is a significant finding, strongly suggesting infection, especially bacterial or tuberculous infection, as microorganisms consume glucose. A high WBC count (1200/cumm) with neutrophil predominance (90%) points towards an inflammatory or infectious process, although the WBC count is not extremely high for a typical bacterial septic arthritis, making tuberculous or other subacute infections more probable. Negative ZN stain does not exclude TB, as it has low sensitivity, especially in paucibacillary conditions like musculoskeletal TB.  However, **positive TB-PCR is a crucial finding, strongly indicating the presence of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* DNA in the synovial fluid and is highly specific for tuberculous infection.**

**Interpret Ancillary Tests - Histopathology:** Histopathology revealing caseating granulomas with epithelioid cells, Langerhans giant cells, and lymphocytic aggregates is pathognomonic for tuberculosis. Caseating granulomas are the hallmark histological feature of TB.

**Integrate and Synthesize all Findings to Formulate Diagnosis:** Combining the clinical presentation (subacute onset of wrist swelling and pain, constitutional symptoms), physical examination (localized wrist inflammation), and ancillary tests (positive Mantoux, lytic lesions on X-ray, TB-PCR positive synovial fluid, and caseating granulomas on histology), the diagnosis of **Tuberculosis of the left wrist joint (Tuberculous arthritis of the wrist)** is definitively established. All findings are highly consistent with this diagnosis. Other differentials like bacterial septic arthritis are less likely due to the subacute onset, constitutional symptoms, and histopathological findings. While fungal infections or other granulomatous diseases could be considered, the positive TB-PCR and caseating granulomas strongly favor tuberculosis.

<Step 2> Rheumatoid Arthritis excluded given the absence of systemic joint involvement, serology for rheumatoid factors, and the presence of TB-specific findings on biopsy.
<Step 3> Osteochondrosis and Bone Tumors initially considered due to lytic lesions on X-ray, but excluded due to confirmed TB-PCR positivity and histological findings.
<Step 4> Pyogenic Osteomyelitis mimicked by chronic inflammatory changes and lytic lesions, but excluded as ZN staining, TB-PCR, and caseating granulomas pointed to TB instead.
<Step 5> Tuberculous Tenosynovitis of the Wrist confirmed due to positive TB-PCR, characteristic caseating granulomas on histopathology, and clinical resolution with anti-TB treatment.

<Step 6> Final diagnosis: Tuberculous Tenosynovitis of the Wrist based on clinical complaints of progressive wrist swelling and pain, constitutional symptoms, radiological findings of lytic lesions, positive TB-PCR, and histopathological confirmation of caseating granulomas.
